user_prompt: I want to run `void test_sdcard()` to test performance of the recorder with encryption enabled
make sure everything is wired so that it works with encryption actually built and active
agent_reply: Wired `test_sdcard()` so it should actually test encrypted recorder performance now.
- `setup()` now calls `all_init()` before bring-up tests, so NVS/M5/gui basics exist before `Aegis` and SD setup.
- installs a deterministic test master key only if no key already exists,
- forces `AudioFileRecorder::setPurePcmMode(false)` so it does not bypass encryption.
- `AudioFileRecorder` now includes `Aegis` by relative path so PlatformIO library discovery works.
- `BtHostList.h` and `WifiManager.h` now include `conf.h`, so their security-mode structs match their `.cpp` files.
Build passed. I restored the generated `src/version.c` bump afterward.

user_prompt: inside `lib\Security\Aegis.h`, provide a constant for a salt that will be used, use "98 c2 5a f2 b7 0f a4 b3 42 b4 64 e5 ee d6 ff 3d 0d d8 21 9c 9d 7b 16 b4 ce de cf fa ca 4e f3 2f " which I randomly generated, we'll use this for now. Also provide a constant for its length
agent_reply: Added `Aegis::kSalt` and `Aegis::kSaltSize` in [Aegis.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Security/Aegis.h:10). It’s a 32-byte `static constexpr uint8_t` array using your generated salt.

user_prompt: `scripts\sectools.py` should have a function that can read a "*.key" file, and return 32 bytes, which is the encryption key
`scripts\sectools.py` should have a main function, using argparse, the user can specify a password, and generates a "*.key" file

By default the "*.key" is 32 bytes, raw binary. If a 64 byte file, or larger, is detected, then attempt to parse it as some sort of hexadecimal string, it could be many formats. The best bet is to remove all "0x", then remove all non alphanumeric characters.
Other file sizes should trigger an fatal error.

`scripts\thefly_audio_decoder.py` should be modified to have an optional specified `*.key` file. If the key is specified, the file MUST use decryption. Implement the decryption function in `scripts\sectools.py`. Feeding in an unencrypted file should result in an error if a key is also specified. If the key is not specified, assume unencrypted file.
The encryption implementation is seen in `lib\AudioFileRecorder\AudioFileRecorder.cpp` so use that as a guide.
agent_reply: - Accepts 64+ byte hex-ish files by stripping `0x` and non-alphanumeric chars, then parsing the first 64 hex chars.
- Fatal errors on other key file sizes.
- CLI derives a `.key` from `--password` using PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 with the filecrypt salt from `Aegis.h`.
- Implements AES-GCM recording decryption for encrypted `.rec` packets.
- If `--key` is supplied, the decoder must decrypt first.
- Unencrypted input with `--key` will fail through encrypted packet size/authentication checks.
- If no key is supplied, behavior stays unencrypted as before.
Also ran small synthetic AES-GCM decrypt and key-parse round trips successfully. Note: `scripts/sectools.py` is showing as untracked in git, even though it existed in your workspace as the TODO file.
